---
title: "AI R Tutorials"
subtitle: "Written with AI — to teach you how to code without leaning on it."
sidebar: ai-curriculum
listing:
  contents:
    - cs-crash-course/index.qmd
  type: default
  sort: title asc
  fields: [title, description]
toc: false
format:
  html:
    page-layout: article
---

<p class="ai-tagline">To understand how to code <em>with</em> AI, you need to understand how to code <em>first</em>. These tracks are for learning R and computer science properly — short quests, real ecology tables, line-by-line comments.</p>

## Choose a track

Use the **Coding curriculum** sidebar to jump to any quest, or open a tab below.

## Functional programming

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Beginner · Base R

**20 quests · ~10 min each**

Vectors, `if`/`else`, `for` loops, the **`apply`** family, **`split` + `lapply`**, and a base R capstone. Start here if dplyr and purrr still feel like magic.

[Open Beginner track →](level-1-base-r/index.qmd)

### Intermediate · dplyr

**20 quests**

`mutate`, `filter`, `group_by`, `lead`/`lag`, joins, pivots — the verbs you use on reef, fishery, and telemetry tables every week.

[Open dplyr track →](level-2-dplyr/index.qmd)

### Intermediate · purrr & furrr

**20 quests**

Lists, **`map`**, **`map2`**, **`safely`**, **`walk`**, and optional parallel **`furrr`**. Finish Base R and most of dplyr first.

[Open purrr track →](level-3-purrr/index.qmd)

### Advanced · data.table

**20 quests**

`[i, j, by]`, `.SD`, joins, **`shift`**, **`fcase`** — when tables get large and you want one expressive syntax.

[Open data.table track →](level-4-data-table/index.qmd)

:::

## Computer science crash course

**20 quests · same format · mostly concepts + light R**

Types, memory, algorithms, debugging, reproducibility, and how to plan an analysis before you write code. Pairs well with any functional programming tab.

[Open CS crash course →](cs-crash-course/index.qmd)

::: {.quest-hub}
<p class="quest-hub__title">100 quests across five modules</p>
<p class="quest-hub__hint"><strong>Human R Tutorials</strong> on this site are full field workflows (telemetry, dive logs). <strong>AI R Tutorials</strong> are the structured curriculum — practice until the pattern is yours, then use any tool you like for speed.</p>
:::

Learning works best in short segments with feedback ([Bransford et al., 2000](https://nap.nationalacademies.org/catalog/9853/how-people-learn-brain-mind-experience-and-school-expanded-edition)). Treat each quest like a lab exercise: predict the output, run the chunk, revise your mental model ([Klahr & Dunbar, 1988](https://doi.org/10.1016/0166-4115(88)90004-2)).
